items.title: Look for round-trip / inverse / idempotence properties first
items.avoid: Reaching for PBT only on obviously algorithmic code.
items.prefer: Apply PBT to any function with `encode/decode`, `parse/serialize`, `normalise`, `sort/rank`.
items.why: These properties are easy to state and high-value — they often catch real bugs in minutes.

items.title: Pin every shrunk bug as an `@example`
items.avoid: Letting Hypothesis re-randomize and possibly miss a known regression.
items.why: Locks in deterministic regression coverage for historical bugs.

items.title: Constrain strategies to your real input domain
items.avoid: Default `st.text()` when your tokenizer doesn't accept surrogate pairs.
items.why: Spending PBT runtime on inputs your code doesn't have to handle is wasted; also generates noise failures.

items.title: Tune `@settings` to the property's importance
items.prefer: Bump `max_examples` for critical properties; lower for cheap ones run frequently.
items.why: More examples = more thorough; balance against CI runtime.

items.title: Use stateful tests for stateful systems
items.avoid: Trying to test caches / indices / queues with one-shot properties.
items.why: Bugs in stateful systems live in operation orderings — only stateful PBT finds them efficiently.
